To this type, the most important thing is being heard. If they feel they've gotten their point across and been fully heard, they are more accepting of alternative outcomes. They are also of the mindset of, well they heard everything I had to say and knew where I was coming from, they just couldn't give me what I wanted. Assertive types hate wasting time. For them, time is money and every wasted minute is a wasted dollar. Their self-image is linked to how many things they can get accomplished in a period of time. They are fiery people who love to feel like they're winning. And as a side note, they feel like they are winning and in control when they are speaking. There is never any confusion about where they stand, since they are so direct and candid. Their style of communication is very aggressive but short-sighted, as they don't worry about future interactions. For them, business relationships are based on respect. Nothing more and nothing less. Since assertive types have such a drive to be heard, they don't really have the ability to listen to you until they know that you've heard them. They are very goal-focused. For assertive types, silence is an opportunity to speak more. Workers work very well on them and accurate summaries aimed at that's right will come more easily. When you're dealing with one of these types, that's right may come in the form of, that's it exactly or you hit the nail on the head. Most times, they are oblivious to expectations of reciprocity because they figure whatever you gave them was only logical. They earned it. They simply look for an opportunity to receive more. Workers will feel like give them an inch and they'll take a mile. Conversely, if they have given a concession, they are counting down the seconds until they get something in return. If you're an assertive personality, number one, be especially conscious of your tone. Your direct tone will interfere with your ability to make great deals. You need to soften your tone and work to make it pleasant. Number two, use labels to build rapport and increase the other side's desire to collaborate. Number three, you see yourself as direct and honest. Your counterpart see you as a loudmouth that is unreasonable. Never be so sure of what you want that you wouldn't take something better. This is the key to flexibility. Assertive types tend to be so focused on what they want that they often miss opportunities to explore other options. Be careful that you don't get tunnel vision. The great strength of the assertive is candor. No one can make a great deal with anyone that will be implemented if they have no idea what the other side needs. You're taking yourself hostage. Click on one of the other options to learn more about the other negotiation types.
